content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for movies where the character talks to themselves or the audience

**Score:** 39
**Body:** I’ve been trying to find more movies where you get to hear the main character's thoughts out loud, kind of like the internal monologue in the show Dexter.
I really like when the narration gives you a look into their head or explains their weird way of seeing the world. Some movies I’ve already seen and liked are 


Fight Club
American Psycho
The Shawshank Redemption
Memento

Does anyone have any other recommendations for movies that use a lot of voice-over? I’m open to pretty much any genre as long as it’s got that vibe. Thanks!

Wow, thanks for all the great suggestions everyone. I've got a massive watchlist now! (Thinks to himself how can I get this into a spreadsheet?)

I've made a list will add more as they come in thanks for all the suggestions.

https://www.reddit.com/r/MovieSuggestions/s/qjVGjr7KaA

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ferris Bueller’s Day Off 
2. Deadpool
3. High Fidelity 
4. Not a movie, but the tv show Fleabag is (imo) one of the best examples of the main character showing you their thoughts. 
5. Goodfellas (1990)  
The Big Short (2015)  
Wayne's World (1992)  
Barry Lyndon (1975)  
Trainspotting (1996)  

6. The Wolf of Wall Street (2013)

Also think the schtick in Sherlock Holmes (2009) and its sequel is fun and might be up your alley.
7. Annie Hall
8. American Beauty
9. Both RDJ Sherlock Holmes movies.

Theatrical cut of Blade Runner
10. The Big Short (2015)

## 2. Lesser known historical dramas

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I love Master and Commander, Kingdom of Heaven, 1917, Legends of the Fall, just to name a few.

Looking for some more obscure dramas that I may have missed!



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Rob Roy is pretty solid. It stars Liam Neeson as Rob Roy McGregor, a Scottish folk hero who was framed by a nobleman and became an outlaw as a result. The nobleman who sets him up is portrayed by Tim Roth . It was released around 1995 .
2. A knight’s tale lol
3. The Long Walk Home

Nixon

Ghosts of Mississippi
4. Wind that Shakes the Barley 
5. The Last Duel

Outlaw King 

The Lost City of Z

 The Return

Ironclad 

Rob Roy

In the Heart of the Sea

6. The Leopard (1963)
7. Enemy At The Gate was really cool
8. ZULU
9. How recent is no longer 'historical'?

The Beast (1988), sometimes listed as The Beast of War, is set during the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan and is one of my favorite war movies of all time, despite being a massive flop that no one's ever heard of.
10. Culloden (1964)

## 3. Educational but fun movies

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I want to watch a bunch of educational movies, but they don’t necesarily have to be documentaries or boring history ones. It can be any topic, based on historical events (like the big short on the financial crisis) or made up but still accurate as long as its a good movie. Preferably some relevant topic or something historical/war related but it doesn’t really matter as long as you can learn something from it. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wag the Dog (1997)
2. Moneyball (2011). It's about baseball, but you don't have to be a baseball or even a sports fan to enjoy it.
3. BlackBerry
4. Emily the Criminal
5. The Post

All the President’s Men

Apollo 13

Bright Star
6. Hidden Figures (2016)
7. Hidden Figures
8. American Made (2017)

It’s the story of Barry Seal smuggling drugs for the CIA. A lot of stuff is dramatized/exaggerated, but it’s accurate enough and an absolute blast to watch. 

9. “Vice”, about Dick Cheney
10. Tora! Tora! Tora!

The Battle of Britain 

The Dam Busters

The Longest Day 

Gettysburg 

Waterloo 



## 4. Movies like The House That Jack Built, Oldboy, I Saw The Devil, Nocturnal Animals, Blue Velvet

**Score:** 12
**Body:** An obscene thriller, all the movies I listed are pretty crazy for higher budget films. Featuring moral depravity and shock value. Prefer when the main character is a weirdo . 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Try more Lars Von Trier - Antichrist (2009) is a good start

Mother! (2017)

Crash (1996)

Pretty much any Yorgos Lanthimos film
2. * Der Goldene Handschuh/The Golden Glove (2019) the portrait of an alcoholic serial killer in 1970s hamburg, very disturbing but well done.
* The Cremator (1969) a controversial but incredible film about a zealous czech crematorium manager who believes that death is the only freedom from earthly suffering, who then is recruited by the nazis. the film is dark but has a sense of humor.
* The Devils (1971) 17th century france, features the incredible Vanessa Redgrave as a sexually repressed nun who accuses a priest of sex magic. all hell breaks loose. wonderful film, incredibly intelligent and political.
* Dead Ringers (1988) Jeremy Irons as identical twin gynecologists who spiral into depravity and madness. Cronenberg's best imo.
3. Rivers Edge (1986) If you want some real weirdo characters then you will really like this one. It's more in the realm of Blue Velvet than the others you listed. Dennis Hopper's character is insane.
4. Peeping Tom (1960)
5. Could check out more Korean flicks: The Chaser, The Man From Nowhere, rest of the Vengeance trilogy now that you've seen Oldboy. 
6. The Golden Glove (2019) and Before The Devil Knows You're Dead
7. Nightcrawler (2014)
8. 'Angst' (1983)
9. Angst (1983)
10. Sheriff Joe

## 5. Looking for something to cheer me up :)

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Hey guys. I had a really crappy day yesterday; I found out my uncle had passed away and then not even an hour later I had a van crash into my car whilst I was driving. (Average Monday morning activities for you.) I really want to watch a film that will cheer me up and get me out of this shitty mood, I’ve got some comfort films I can watch but would like to try and watch some new ones too. 

Thanks :)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. BABE (The pig, not the baseball player)
2. The Birdcage, Best in Show, Moonstruck, The Replacements, The Cutting Edge, Saved!, When Harry Met Sally, Young Frankenstein 

These all make me happy.  I gave a range since I'm not sure what your tastes are.
3. Two movies that start off low but end up making me come out of my depressive hole:

Chef (2014)

The Secret Life of Walter Mitty (2013)

Both are feel good endings. Hope you feel better soon!

Edit: typo
4. Joe vs the Volcano
5. Ferris Burller's Day Off
6. Strictly Ballroom 
7. Marcel The Shell With Shoes On is a warm hug of a movie
8. Sound of music will have you cry but also feel a bit better. Princess bride also

What kind of movies do you like?
9. Planes, Trains & Automobiles (1987)
10. Tampopo is my happy movie

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What is your favorite random cameo is a movie?

**Score:** 1154

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. David Bowie in Zoolander
2. ![gif](giphy|zKqif3trvJPCo)
  - Tatum was also hilarious in his small role in Bullet Train 
3. Matt Damon as theater Loki 😂
4. ![gif](giphy|bxpKtZZmCTxnK6k2Fh|downsized)
  - Her Majesty is fucking hard to beat
    - It was great as it was. But I still wish she'd had a corgi in her lap, she turned and said "ah mister Bond. I've been expecting you."
5. I thought it was funny how they had a high paid actor like Brad Pitt in Deadpool 2 and kill him off almost immediately.
  - Then they had a high paid actor like Ryan Reynolds in Bullet Train just to roast him the entire time for calling in sick and have him on screen for 30 seconds
6. Keith richards as jack sparrows father...

## 2. Which movie couples have chemistry so real it feels like you’re intruding?

**Score:** 952
**Body:** My pick: Jesse & Celine from the Before Trilogy. 

What are your favourite examples of on-screen couples with chemistry SO good it feels like you’re watching two real people fall in love? 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not romantically, but Brad Pitt and George Clooney has insane on-screen chemistry. 

I mean, the tease trailer for Wolfs was just 20 seconds of them sitting in a car not saying a word and it still gave off major I-wanna-watch-this-vibes 

(too bad the movie sucked) 
  - You could see it in the Ocean's Trilogy. They're basically reading each other's minds lol
2. Gosling and Emma Stone in crazy stupid love. 
3. I know I’m going hard off topic here, but hear me out:

Riggs and Murtaugh
  - Especially the bathroom scene 
4. Adam Sandler & Drew Barrymore - The Wedding Singer and 50 First Dates 
5. When Harry met Sally
6. Brad and Angelina in Mr and Mrs Smith, Eva Green and Daniel Craig in Casino Royale come to mind. Although the former is a bit more… apparent as to why
7. John and Ygritte in GOT
  - That belongs in the next level cue of “Chemistry SO good, the actors got married irl” 😭 

## 3. Which film and performance made you a fan of Paul Giamatti?

**Score:** 632
**Body:** Performance: John Adams

Film: Big Fat Liar

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Holdovers is one of my comfort films
2. John Adams is annual watching for me 
3. Shoot em up
4. Sideways love that film. 
5. Pig vomit
  - W Nnnnn B C
6. Cinderella Man
7. American Splendor. 
It also made me go through a Harvey Pekar phase.

I grew out of that Harvey phase, but love Paul more everyday
8. sideways, but he's one of those actors i could watch fix a drain pipe, like anthony hopkins or steve zahn

or sidney sweeney...
  - Steve Zahn doesn't get enough love or recognition. 

## 4. Is a Knight of the seven Kingdoms worth watching?

**Score:** 417
**Body:** I’ve never watched the game of thrones series, I haven’t even seen clips of it, however I’ve heard this show is amazing but do you need to know the GOT series to enjoy it? Is it really that good or is it overhyped. Let me know!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Its fun. Much lighter. Really enjoyed it. 
  - It’s going to get intense at some point. Duncan the tall has 4 pages in the kings guard book, it’s an Easter egg in game of thrones (Joffrey is talking to Jaime about how little he has accomplished and mentions “sir Duncan the tall” and filling four pages while Jaime has barely half a page.)
2. Short answer: yes

Long answer: yeeeeeeeeeeeeeessssssssssssss
  - ![gif](giphy|2GjLggbysBQUt15lB0|downsized)
3. Indeed 
4. Yes
5. You don't need to know much about game of thrones to enjoy it. It's set many years before and it has a simpler, and very good story and characters 
6. I was not a big fan of House of the Dragon but Knight of the seven Kingdoms really pulled me back to GOT. 
7. They filmed it on real locations, outdoors, and built sets. Instead of shooting everything on a green screen in a studio, a la The Mandalorian. 

And you can feel it in every frame. That alone makes it worth watching.
8. Not just yes, but fuck yes.  The nice part is that you'll get through it quick.  The downside is, you'll get through it quick

## 5. Project Hail Mary (2026) A cinematic masterpiece

**Score:** 192
**Body:** Had the chance to watch Project Hail Mary last week and as a huge lover of the book, having read it from cover to cover countless times I was cautiously optimistic. What I wasn't prepared for was a sci-fi movie of a generation on the level of movies like Interstellar that will be talked about for decades. Incredible from start to finish and one of the best book to screen adaptations we've had in years. Anyone have plans to go see it?

Completely spoiler free full review here for those interested [https://adamreviewsfilm.com/project-hail-mary/](https://adamreviewsfilm.com/project-hail-mary/)

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Can you read the review (it has spoilers so I didnt read it) on AV Club and tell me what you disagree with? They seem to like-but-not-love it, giving it a solid B. 

I loved the book. 
  - Just read it, and 75% of the review is about what happens in the movie rather than what makes it good/bad. I could be wrong, but it seems like it's been written by someone who hasn't read the book so not all the comedy lands. Other than that though, there isn't really any negative language, so not sure why it's been pumped down to a B rather than A-/B+
2. I haven't watched it yet. I never read the book and *was* looking forward to the movie, but I'm still salty that they gave away so much in the trailers.

I'll still watch it when it hits streaming services.
  - Fucking hate trailers nowadays. 

I read the book. I closed my eyes when the trailer came up about 1/3rd through to not have the entire reveal and movie spoiled - and that's with knowing the entire story. 
    - I still don't get the hate - how is the alien reveal such a big thing (at least I don't remember it being "a twist" in the book - and certainly not "the big twist").
      - It surprised me as much as the main character
  - I read the book four times….They didn’t give much in the trailers. 

Go see it. You’ll love it. 
3. The trailers looked mid as fk. Hopefully the final product is as good as you said
  - I agree, I was expecting the worse looked way too cheesy, the movie however is entirely different thankfully!
4. I've been excited about this since I finished the book and was told there would be a movie.  
It's the first movie I've wanted to see in theaters since Dune II came out.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Is there a Middle Hollywood between Old and New?

**Score:** 40
**Body:** While we tend to think of 1967 as the dividing line between Old and New Hollywood, I think it's pretty clear that there was an intermediate generation (Middle Hollywood?) of filmmakers who were born in the 1920s, made their theatrical debuts in the 1950s(for the most part), and made provocative, boundary-pushing films in the first half of the 1960s. Stanley Kubrick, of course, but also Sidney Lumet, John Frankenheimer, Arthur Penn, Frank Perry, various Roger Corman joints and pioneers of American independent cinema like John Cassavetes and Michael Roehmer.

If you think of, say, Lolita, Dr. Strangelove, The Pawnbroker, Fail-Safe, The Manchurian Candidate, The World's Greatest Sinner and Nothing But a Man (which all came out in a three-year period), you have a half-dozen films that feel very different than the Old Hollywood of the fifties. Films that really feel like the beginning of what we'd call New Hollywood.

You also have the Film-Makers' Cooperative/New American Cinema Group of experimental filmmakers like Stan Brakhage, Andy Warhol and Shirley Clarke who will also impact sixties counterculture.

r/truefilm, is there an argument for either

* pushing our narrative of New Hollywood's begin back five or six years or
* Conceptualizing these filmmakers and their films as a distinct movement in American cinema?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Who’s Afraid of Virginia Woolf probably fits here, too, even though Mike Nichols next film (The Graduate) is associated with new Hollywood. As for your bigger question, I think there is a tendency to mythologize cultural history  and identify some critical break with the past so that we can tell a simpler and more compelling story, but the truth isn’t so organized. Similar to how history pretends that everything before Nirvana was hair metal and ignores a ton of similar music slowly making its way out from all the underground scenes of the 80s.
  - I agree with your main point. Splitting history into periods is always somewhat arbitrary. To continue with the music analogy, you always have REM and Pixies and Jane's Addiction and Sonic Youth and so forth that predate the "official" beginning of the alt rock boom.

Mike Nichols is an interesting name to bring up because, while Who Afraid of Virginia Woolf was his first film, he'd already built a legacy as a groundbreaking comedian and satirist in multiple media.

In the early sixties he definitely was a very trendy, influential cultural figure, even if not as a filmmaker.
    - Yeah, you don’t get Liz Taylor and Richard Burton in your first movie unless you are already making waves. I think he was friends with Taylor before. And, Sandy Dennis plays Honey kinda how you’d expect Elaine May to play the character. 
      - Is there anyone else you think would fit into this loosely defined category? 
2. There’s one model of looking at Hollywood history where the postwar period is a postclassical phase that is, essentially, a middle ground between old and new. Many films of this era, from the many films noir showing the dark underside of America, to mildly deconstructive genre films like The Searchers, Liberty Valance, or The Ox Bow Incident, to social problem melodramas like Ace in the Hole or Bigger Than Life, were cautiously experimental with the formulas of classical Hollywood. They weren’t fully transgressive or deconstructive yet, and often provided superficially happy endings, but they played with Hollywood convention and quietly undermined the themes of its most popular genres.

New Hollywood would then take those inclinations much further by going fully deconstructive, but the late ‘40s through the early ‘60s had a lot of forerunners that were pushing boundaries as much as the Hollywood institution would allow.
  - I guess what I'm arguing is that I'm not sure you can talk about pre-1967 postwar American cinema as on undivided whole.

While there's obvious a lot of continuity, I think there is a distinct c.1957-1966 younger auteur/auteur-adjacent aesthetic that feels very different than, say, early 50s Hollywood. Dr. Strangelove, Nothing But a Man, A Hard Day's Night feel very distinctly 1964 and not at all like movies that could have been made a decade earlier. 


3. A few weeks ago, someone made a post that got removed. Without a title anymore, I can’t remember exactly what they were asking, but it might have been about how to better assure you’re interested in the films you pick to watch. I described how a lot of what I’ve watched in the past year has had some relation to the You Must Remember This podcast series called The Old Man is Still Alive, about over a dozen classical era directors as they hung on into changing times. 

In another comment, you ask for names of directors whose works one might look at in considering an in-between an Old and New Hollywood. I’ll just copy and paste from that previous comment the names of directors I’ve been looking at (16 directors here, I think; additionally the Karina Longworth podcast discusses 14 classical era directors):

>Fred Zinneman, Elia Kazan, Joseph L. Mankiewicz, Stanley Kubrick, [Douglas Sirk], [Anthony Mann], Stanley Kramer, Sidney Lumet, Richard Brooks, Blake Edwards, John Sturges, Budd Boetticher, Norman Jewison, Franklin A. Schaffner, Arthur Penn, Mike Nichols, Don Siegel, Jack Clayton, Robert Rossen, Roman Polanski.

John Huston would be an interesting figure to consider. He made films seemingly in the classical style and in a postclassical style, but did he really significantly change his approach to material or filming all that much over his career?

1961: Breakfast at Tiffany’s — pure classical Hollywood, or something new?

I think Lolita is a big deal. I think Billy Wilder tries to chase both these films with Kiss Me, Stupid (set in a town called Climax, and pretty wild in its material) and Irma La Douce (though I haven’t watched this film yet).

Also Psycho and Marnie are interesting movies which suggest something transitional. Way back in 1950 there was Sunset Boulevard.
  - >John Huston would be an interesting figure to consider. He made films seemingly in the classical style and in a postclassical style, but did he really significantly change his approach to material or filming all that much over his career?

I'd say absolutely.

He debuts with The Maltese Falcon. Then you have his military service and his groundbreaking wartime documentary work. He wins Best Director and Best Adapted Screenplay for The Treasure of the Sierra Madre, a film that stands out in Old Hollywood for its extensive location shoot & starts one of his trademark auteur motifs: films shot on location in 'exotic' locales around the world.

In the 50s, you have his 8 collaborations with DP Oswald Morris that represent a really new, experimental approach to cinematography via fog filters, gels, color grading, etc. The 50s also sees a pattern of Huston bringing non-screenwriters (James Agee, Ray Bradbury, Truman Capote) to Hollywood as writing partners.

The 60s is kind of his 'lost decade' but he completely reinvents himself in 1972 with Fat City, a very grounded, gritty, realistic New Hollywood-style drama.

Finally, his last film The Dead feels very different than anything else in his filmography. It's almost an entire film of people in one location talking and really feels like the perfect example of a pared-down, introspective **late style**.

TLDR: he's definitely a filmmaker who changed his approach multiple times throughout his career.

I don't really think of him as proto-New Hollywood, though. He was born in 1906 and working as a Hollywood screenwriter in the 30s. Career-wise and family-wise, he had deep roots in Old Hollywood.
4. This is certainly a tantalizing claim. There was definitely a discernible shift in form and content from the Old Hollywood in the late 50s and early 60s. I think this had largely to do with Hollywood starting to push back on the Hays Code. Preminger led that charge. Television arrived and it was a viable threat to the movie business. So, pushing the content envelope was one way of getting people back into the theaters. 

It was also a time when the business model of movie stars being chained to exclusive studio contracts started to show cracks. You had actors like Burt Lancaster starting their own production companies. I think that contributed to the general shift i.e. more challenging subject matter, "smarter" films.

Ultimately, I think the whole "New Hollywood" period was really more ideological in spirit than it was about "auteurism" in the studio system. There was a cultural shift in the late 60s with the younger generation: anti-establishment, civil rights, cynicism towards the government, the sexual revolution, etc. So, it's no mere accident that Easy Rider and Bonnie And Clyde are the films cited for launching the New Hollywood.

So, I think therein lies the distinction. Those early 60s films like Fail Safe, Manchurian Candidate reflected the "doom and gloom" of a time when Cold War paranoia was prevalent.  Also, think of those same films you mentioned and you can view them as "adult" fare. Post-1967, the key films that launched the New Hollywood attracted a whole new generation of younger viewers. 

Finally, the 60s was the golden age of foreign cinema in the US. The French New Wave, Fellini, Antonioni (see: Blow Up), Bergman, Kurosawa influenced that New Hollywood generation. You see it  more prevalent in the aesthetics post 1967ish. 
  - Some good points.

The other big international influence to add (that a lot of people seem to forget about) is the British New Wave/angry young men/kitchen sink realism/whatever you want to call it, which did have quite an impact on American cinema. 

## 2. Any recommendations for great film criticism/theory books?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Looking for some more outlets on film literature and wanted to see if there were any hidden gems I’ve missed. I would say one aspect I’m probably most interested too is the history of film and the behind the scenes of how some movies got made. Also a big horror fan and am endlessly fascinated by that genre and its connection/mirror to real life events. Thank you in advance! 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hands down the best book on film history is David Cook's History of Narrative Film. It's the most widely used text in college film history courses. 

The best book on film aesthetics - at the introductory level - is "Film Art: An Introduction" by Bordwell and Thompson. Again, this, too, is widely used as the introductory text for film analysis in most introductory film studies classes.  

A more intermediate book is "Film Theory: An Introduction" edited by Braudy and Cohen. It's a comprehensive look at the major film theories. And from its bibliography you could then do a deeper dive into each critical approach.

For a less academic approach: Peter Biskind's "Easy Riders, Raging Bulls" and 'Down And Dirty Pictures" are great tellings of the film business from the 60s to the 80s.

Also, William Goldman's "Aventures In The Screen Trade" is a classic. It looks at Hollywood from a screenwriter's perspective and also touches upon the craft itself. You don't have to be a screenwriter but you gain a lot of understanding about that aspect of filmmaking.
2. I cannot recommend “House of Psychotic Women” by Kier-La Janisse highly enough. It is easily the best book on horror films that I have. It’s also a deeply personal bold creative and unique all-in book of film criticism and theory that I’ve found myself returning to many times. When you read the work of a really great film critic/theorist after you’ve viewed or rewatched something interesting, it’s like having the best post movie conversation you could hope to with your smartest cinephile friend. 
3. I like *Men, Women and Chainsaws: Gender and Modern Horror* by Carol J. Clover. It's the origin for the term "final girl" in horror. Carol J. Clover's books before this were historical works of medieval scholarship, but this is a feminist anthropological study of horror films focused primarily on the rape-revenge subgenre in horror. He argument is that horror is a way for men to sympathize with women who experience gendered violence. 
  - this book wuz in conversation with Siskel & Ebert’s rants against the slasher film. Will forever make u look at the original “I Spit on Your Grave” in a different way.
4. Andre Bazin's books What is Cinema? 1 and 2 and Andrew Sarris' The American Cinema are both quite important historically. Sarris' book is fundamental in establishing the auteur theory.

I took a class over the winter on the books Cinema 1: the movement image and Cinema 2: the time image, by Gilles Deleuze. They're extremely interesting but very difficult to read. 

I'm reading a collection of essays just now called Philosophy of Film Noir, edited by Mark Conard, that's been a fantastic and very accessible read. There's a neo noir one as well

Slavoj Zizek is a great modern philosopher and psychoanalyst who's written quite a bit on film, any of his stuff would be worth checking out but the easiest thing to do is watch his documentary called The Pervert's Guide to Cinema which is available for free on YouTube (along with his other one, The Pervert's Guide to Ideology).

Another documentary, The Story of Film: An Oddysey by Irish critic Mark Cousins. Its a 15 hour epic documentary that covers all major innovative moments in film history in chronological order. Could not reccomend this one enough
5. Resubmitting for length requirements.

For books, I'd HIGHLY suggest the new book, *Purging the Devil*, which is about exorcisms. Sounds right up your alley. It deals with a lot of media portrayals and is unsurprisingly focused on \*The Exorcist\* and the "realness" of the phenomenon. For a more "classic" text, though not directly film theory, is Mark Fisher's seminal work *Capitalist Realism, which* uses some film analysis to discuss his philosophical observations. Most notably, he uses \*Children of Men\* as the basis for his famous quote, "It's easier to imagine the end of the world than it is to imagine the end of capitalism."

For movies that do what you are looking for, I have three for ya. Zizek's *Pervert's Guide to Cinema* and its sequel *Pervert's Guide to Ideology* are great and are a philosophical approach to what you're looking for.

The other interesting rec for you is the film *Room 237* about Kubrick's *The Shining*. It's not a true critical analysis by any stretch of the imagination, but there is a lot of that interwoven with more conspiratorial interpretations as well as a lot of discussion about the production of the film. The style of narration is cool imo, it's very trance-like, and I like the post-modern presentation of how many different perspectives on the film and Kubrick's motives there are.
  - Thank you! The Zizek one sounds very interesting… love his writing 
    - Oh if you already know and like him Pervert’s Guide is a MUST watch. Just get ready for sniffling LOL.
6. The Major Film Theories: An Introduction by J Dudley Andrew 

More of a history of film theory….gives intros to various philosophies over time. From the 70s, so you get nothing past that, but it’s an interesting read. 

## 3. Forgotten movies.

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Recently, I was recommended a Dutch film made in 1982 called "de smaak van water." When I looked into it I found that there is no platform who carries this film, nor was there ever a release to VHS or Dvd. The film in question did receive some awards and nominations upon release, but quickly fell into oblivion. Most of this seems to be caused by a small market, limited cinematic release and distribution rights disputes. It seems that soms national archives still have copies of the film, but it remains to be seen how accessible those are to the public.

  
When looking into it, I found that several movies suffered the same fate. Here's a list of some examples:

* De smaak van water (1982)
* Gebroken spiegels (1984)
* De grens (1984)
* Blonde Dolly (1987)
* kracht (1990)

I was wondering if anyone has any tips for finding movies such as these. It pains my heart that those movies are already, almost forgotten. Currently, it seems that my best bet is to get in touch with the archives.

Does anyone know how regularly this occurs with smaller films? Is this limited to some countries or is it more of an industry wide problem?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Unfortunately for these movies your only source is probably going the way of the high seas, and one particular site in question which is very tricky to get into but probably has the most extensive movie library ever put together on the internet. Being a member, I can tell you that *De smaak van water* got a vhs release, and all the others got a dvd release except for *De grens* which seems to be really forgotten. *Gebroken spiegels* even have a BR release.
  - *De grens* at least has a request, so someone out there is thinking about it!
  - Are you talking the one that starts with K? That's where I found three but was unable to locate Blonde Dolly!
    - Mine starts with P...
      - Ahhh I was wondering about that! Good to know its superior! Thanks.
2. Of the five you listed I was able to find the first two and last. Broken Mirrors had a blu-ray release in 2023, kracht just had a 4k restoration in 2024, and I was able to find a vhs copy of De Smaak van water. 

All are also piratable as well of course. 

Couldn't find Blonde Dolly or De Grens though!

## 4. What do you think is the best 70s Disaster Film?

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Question, What do you think is the best 70s Disaster Film? and explain why?

To me, I think it is The Poseidon Adventure, It was the right amount of action, right amount of disaster and a great all star cast led by Gene Hackman, Ernest Borgnine, Shelley Winters, Jack Albertson, Red Buttons, Roddy McDowell, and Stella Stevens. I just really enjoy this film and it is just a fun watch. I will also say that The Towering Inferno isn't far behind, and I like just as much as The Poseidon Adventure.

For other Disaster films in the 70s there is The Airport Series, Skyjacked, Earthquake, Juggernaut, The Hindenburg, The Cassandra Crossing, Two Minute Warning, Rollercoaster, The Swarm, Gray Lady Down, Avalanche, Beyond The Poseidon Adventure, Meteor, City On Fire, and When Time Ran Out

So, What do you think is the best 70s Disaster Film?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I really really liked Black Sunday. It has a great script, cast and director by all rights it should have been a box office smash and well talked about to this day but another film was released on the same weekend named 'Star Wars'  and that changed the face of cinema while Black Sunday was relegated to footnote. 
  - Great book
2. A couple years ago I watched the entire Airport series in reverse chronological order. The first one isn't really even a disaster film. It's more of an airport procedural. It's also the only one that's really any good. They got increasingly ridiculous from one to the next.

The other disaster films on your list are merely memories from my childhood, but the one that stands out is *The Towering Inferno*, and one not on your list, *The China Syndrome*. 

BTW, the Wikipedia has a pretty comprehensive list:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_disaster_films

Just 70s: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:1970s_disaster_films
  - Airport is so strange. I know it gets credited for starting the trend but if I’m remembering right there’s only 1 death in the whole film. The first hour is entirely a marital issue drama that like, happens to involve weather that could become a problem for the airport but never actually causes one. 
    - Yup, >!only the mentally ill guy who hijacks the plane dies in a suicide that he had wanted to disguise in order to pay out a life insurance policy to his wife.!< But that's almost a side story. A lot of the film is centered around Lancaster trying to keep the airport running during a snowstorm.
      - This is why I think The Poseidon Adventure is the true start of the Disaster Film Genre.
        - You could arguably credit it for bringing the genre to prominence in the 1970s, but disaster films are [nearly as old as film](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fire!_(1901_film\)).
3. We just did a comparison between *The Poseidon Adventure* and *The Towering Inferno* for my podcast. I actually preferred *Inferno* to *Poseidon* even though the latter I think is a stronger “local drama” — basically a chamber piece complete with shepherd, flock, and sheep. It almost precludes the whole “Moral Majority” evangelical book that’d get big in the following decade, and so feels more traditional and familiar for it.

But *Inferno* is unlike so many movies made today! I mean that primarily in scale… yes, the sets and special effects are huge, but the whole thing feels like an ant farm. We get this awesome cross-section of this Tower of Babel on fire with a cast of mostly just people. There are some “main character,” but not in the same way as *Poseidon*. It’s almost Eisensteinian in its focus on the collective. We just don’t see a lot of filmmaking like that nowadays in Hollywood.
4. Omega Man (1971). 

Great lead, great villain. Enormously slow build up. Expressed existent political/racial tensions (which oddly are mirrored some today). VERY 70s aura. Scared the shit out of me a kid...in a good way. 

## 5. A Condensed Global History of Cinema

**Score:** 13
**Body:** Here is the list on Letterboxd - https://boxd.it/SX7Ts/detail

For about a year I’ve been working away at making a comprehensive list of all film movements, both officially recognized as well as emerging or underrepresented in discourse. It also has industry trends, genre waves, and other important cultural periods of film history that isn’t strictly a film movement. It’s a pretty large and unwieldy list for most I would think, so recently I’ve made a companion list that has three films for each section. I wanted to share it and get feedback, primarily on any important film movements or trends that I’ve over-looked (I have about 10 that I’m currently considering and curating). 

I know some of the sections aren’t perfect, for example there are some large and overly broad sections such as the Post-WWII European Art House which I think needs work. There are some I want to split into more distinct sections such as the Martial Arts into possibly a Shaw Brothers/Golden Harvest or a wave 1 and wave 2. Similarly I think I should separate the Chinese 5th and 6th Generations. Just as a few examples. 

Anyways, let me know what you think and please let me know what suggestions you would make.

2. I want to give this a measured response and real thought. It’ll sound critical but this is a big ambitious thing you’re doing so I think it’s worth sharing a perspective. 

You’re right about large and overly broad sections and you identified one of the main ones, that European postwar section is less a real movement of films in conversation with each other or with the aftermath of the war and seems like more of a collection of “these films seem too important to leave off the list but I can’t justify them as any other movement”. Other categories I feel that way about are your Iranian new wave (just the entire history of Iranian cinema? Should surely be broken up with a first group of the beginnings, a break at the revolution, and picking up modern Iranian cinema about the time of Kiarostami) and American independent cinema (too long of a time span to really be a connected thing, this should probably have a group A of the Miramax era that ends when the Weinsteins left to form their own company, and a group B after that). The American blockbuster category ironically makes sense despite being among the longest timespans of anything on the list, seeing as the most popular movies of the 2010s and 2020s are so often in conversation with the most popular movies of the 1970s and 80s. I think having a “Disney Transitional Era” that overlaps with the Disney Renaissance is inherently silly, surely the timeline for that should start after the end of the renaissance. What would they be transitioning from otherwise?

Specific movie gripe, The Conjuring is not found footage, Blumhouse, or low budget/independent. That is mid-budget studio horror which was released as a summer blockbuster. 

Do you plan on doing companion writing for this? Or is that already baked into your reviews of the films (which I admit I read none of)? Because the inherent problem with this sort of project is that the bigger and more unwieldy the list gets, the less value it has as just a list. If you give me a list of 15 cool movies to check out, I might actually check them out. If you give me a list of 1500 movies, I’ll think it’s neat that someone put in the effort to compile all of that but never actually read through it front to back or refer to it when deciding what I want to watch. 
  - I appreciate the detailed and thoughtful response. I make lists like these as a way for me to learn more about film history and find new things to watch. I do plan on writing a little bit about each “movement” as I learn more about them and feel more confident (and have the time).

There has definitely been a pull to not miss important films. In fact I have a collection of about 200 films I have on a private list of important and influential films that I’m looking at to see if there are any trends that can reasonably justify including the film. 

I agree with all of the specific points you raised and a lot were already non consideration. As to the Disney point: the Disney Renaissance is a specific thing. It encapsulates the reemergence of their 2D animation into a place of critical and financial success. The transitional period, though clunky, is meant to primarily focus on the live action disney projects and show the evolution from smaller / medium budget family films to more Disney cast focused vehicles. I also included the transition between the less successful 2D stuff that falls outside the renaissance era that led directly to Disney prioritizing 3D animation. That admittedly might be a mistake, but it’s a small yet important part of animation history that I hope to keep intact on the list in some way.
